flashcards.Back: An architectural style for creating web services that use standard HTTP methods to interact with resources. it is the most common API type for network automation.
The four basic functions of persistent storage: Create, Read, Update, and Delete. These are mapped to specific HTTP verbs in REST APIs.
The HTTP verb used to 'Read' or retrieve information from a resource. it is non-destructive and does not modify data on the server.
The HTTP verb used to 'Create' a new resource or data structure on the server. it is typically used to add a new configuration entry.
The HTTP verb used to 'Update' or replace an entire existing resource with new data. if the resource doesn't exist, it may create it.
An HTTP verb used for partial updates to a resource. unlike PUT, it only modifies the specific fields provided in the request.
The HTTP verb used to 'Delete' or remove a specified resource from the server, such as deleting a VLAN or a user account.
The specific string of characters used to identify a resource in a REST API, such as https://controller/api/v1/interfaces.
The specific object or piece of data being manipulated via the API, such as a switch port, a routing entry, or a device status.
The process of converting complex data structures (like variables in a script) into a text format (like JSON or XML) for transmission over a network.
JavaScript Object Notation. A lightweight, human-readable data format that uses key-value pairs and is the standard for most modern REST APIs.
eXtensible Markup Language. A data encoding format that uses hierarchical tags (like HTML) to define data elements. it is used by NETCONF.
A REST characteristic where the server does not store any client context between requests. Each API call must contain all the information needed to process it.
The fundamental REST architecture where the client (automation script) makes requests and the server (network controller) provides responses.
The part of an API request that carries metadata, such as authentication tokens, content-type (JSON/XML), and the desired HTTP verb.
The actual data content sent in an API request (e.g., in a POST or PUT) or received in a response, typically formatted as JSON.
A temporary security credential (like a Bearer token) provided in an API header to prove that the client has permission to access the resource.
A 3-digit number returned by the server to indicate the result of an API call. e.g., 200 (Success), 404 (Not Found), or 201 (Created).
Optional fields added to the end of a URI (starting with '?') used to filter or sort the data returned by an API GET request.
The initial part of an API address that stays the same for all calls to that specific service (e.g., https://api.cisco.com/dnac).
The final part of a URI that points to a specific functional area or resource type (e.g., /network-device or /vlan).
A property where making the same API request multiple times (like PUT or DELETE) results in the same final state on the server.
A mandatory HTTP header field that tells the server how the data in the request body is formatted (e.g., application/json).
The practice of including a version number in the URI (like /v1/ or /v2/) to ensure that scripts don't break when the API is updated.
A REST constraint where responses must define themselves as cacheable or not to improve network efficiency and reduce server load.
The REST principle that simplifies the architecture by requiring all clients to use the same standardized methods to interact with the server.
A REST characteristic where a client cannot tell if it is connected directly to the end server or through an intermediary like a load balancer.
The encryption protocols used by HTTPS to protect API communication from eavesdropping and tampering.
A simple API security method where the username and password are encoded in Base64 and sent in the HTTP header.
A popular software tool used by network engineers to easily send REST API calls (GET, POST, etc.) from automation scripts.
